name: mitoinstaller
documentation: The mitoinstaller package allows you to install the mitosheet package in your local JupyterLab instance. It logs anonymous data about installation, including if it is successful or why it failed.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.create_startup_file.create_startup_file
documentation:
Adds a file import_mitosheet.py to the user's IPython startup folder 
so that Mito is automatically imported everytime they start an IPython kernel. 

This allows us to display a button in the user's pandas dataframe output that 
lets them open the dataframe in Mito.

modules.name: mitoinstaller.commands
documentation:
Contains useful commands for interacting
with the command line directly

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.get_output
documentation: NOTE: Do not import subprocess here, we only want one
function to have it

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.run_command
documentation:
An internal command that should be used to run all commands
that run on the command line, so that output from failing
commands can be captured.

Can toggle if this raises an error with fail_on_nonzero_exit_code

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.jupyter_labextension_list
documentation:
Returns the stdout, stderr pair for the currently
installed jupyterlab extensions.

This may error if the user does not have JLab installed.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.uninstall_labextension
documentation: Uninstall a labextension

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.uninstall_pip_packages
documentation:
This function uninstalls the given packages in a single pass
using pip, through the command line.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.install_pip_packages
documentation:
This function installs the given packages in a single pass
using pip, through the command line.

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12332975/installing-python-module-within-code

If you want to install from TestPyPu, just set test_pypi to true

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.upgrade_mito_installer
documentation: Upgrades the mito installer package itself

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.check_running_jlab_3_processes
documentation:
Returns true if there are running JLab 3 processes, 
returns false if there are not.

Useful for telling the user to refresh their servers
if they install Mito

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.check_running_jlab_not_3_processes
documentation:
Returns true if there are running JLab processes, 
returns false if there are not.

Useful for telling the user to refresh their servers
if they install Mito

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.check_running_jlab_processes
documentation:
Returns true if there are running JLab processes from any version
returns false if there are not.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.get_extension_names_from_labextension_list_output
documentation:
Returns a list of the extension names installed from the stdout, stderr
pair returned from the jupyter labextension list.

If you run the command: `jupyter labextension list`, the output is: 
------
JupyterLab v0.35.0
Known labextensions:
app dir: /Users/nate/saga-vcs/monorepo/mito/installer/venv/share/jupyter/lab
        @jupyter-widgets/jupyterlab-manager v0.38.1 enabled OK
------

Note that part of this output prints to stdout, and other parts to stderr 
(for some reason), so we append them with a newline so we make sure we 
get all of the extensions correctly!

functions.name: mitoinstaller.commands.get_jupyterlab_metadata
documentation:
Helper function that returns a tuple of: jupyterlab_version, installed_extensions

If no JupyterLab is installed, returns (None, None)

functions.name: mitoinstaller.starter_notebook.try_create_starter_notebook
documentation:
Creates MITO_STARTER_NOTEBOOK_CONTENTS at MITO_STARTER_NOTEBOOK_PATH,
which helps the user get started.

If the file already exists, will not replace it, as to not overwrite
users work in this area.

modules.name: mitoinstaller.log_utils
documentation:
Useful functions and utilities for
logging information about install.

Also utils for printing information
to the user.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.log_utils.is_on_kuberentes_mito
documentation: Returns True if the user is on Kuberentes Mito, on staging or on app

functions.name: mitoinstaller.log_utils.is_local_deployment
documentation:
Helper function for figuring out if this a local deployment or a
Mito server deployment

functions.name: mitoinstaller.log_utils.identify
documentation:
This identify call identifies the user in our logs. Note that
we make sure that the actual identify call only runs when
the user has a user.json with only a static id, as if we
have more feilds, then the user has already run the mitosheet.sheet
call and thus been identified there.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.log_utils.get_recent_traceback
documentation:
Helper function that returns the most recent traceback, with the file paths
stripped to remove all but the mitosheet file names for ease in debugging.

Inspired by https://stackoverflow.com/questions/25272368/hide-file-from-traceback

functions.name: mitoinstaller.log_utils.log_error
documentation:
Logs an error by adding the traceback to the error, for easier 
debugging.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.log_utils._get_experiment_params
documentation:
Get data relevant for tracking the experiment, so we can 
see how the experiment is running

NOTE: this must match the function in mitosheet

functions.name: mitoinstaller.log_utils.log
documentation: A utility that all logging should pass through

functions.name: mitoinstaller.experiments.experiment_utils.get_random_variant
documentation:
Returns "A" or "B" with 50% probability

Currently, as we have no experiment, only returns B

modules.name: mitoinstaller.__main__
documentation:
The Mito Installer package contains utils for installing
Mito within your Python environment.

Long term, we aim to meet:
1. This package has minimal dependencies, both for speed of download and the ultimate portability.
2. The installation attempts to fail as early as possible, and to give the user as much help
   help as possible while doing so.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.__main__.main
documentation:
The main function of the Mito installer, this function is responsible
for installing and upgrading the `mitosheet` package.

To install Mito:
python -m mitoinstaller install

To upgrade Mito:
python -m mitoinstaller upgrade

To install Mito from TestPyPi
python -m mitoinstaller install --test-pypi

functions.name: mitoinstaller.jupyter_utils.is_jupyter_notebook_running
documentation:
Format of command output: 

Currently running servers:
http://localhost:8888/?token=wenflwenflqnwdlkqmwldkm :: Path/to/launch

functions.name: mitoinstaller.user_install.get_random_id
documentation:
Creates a new random ID for the user, which for any given user,
should only happen once.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.user_install.is_running_test
documentation:
A helper function that quickly returns if the current code is running inside 
of a test, which is useful for making sure we don't generate tons of logs

functions.name: mitoinstaller.user_install.get_current_experiment
documentation: Returns the current experiment object, or None if it does not exist

functions.name: mitoinstaller.user_install.user_json_is_installer_default
documentation:
Returns True if the user.json file is the installer default, 
and otherwise returns False. 

This allows us to not call identify if we have already done
so in the mitosheet package (which would overwrite things
we don't want to).

functions.name: mitoinstaller.installer_steps.initial_installer_steps.initial_install_step_add_env_for_which_jupyter
documentation:
This install steps checks, up front, which very of jupyter we should
launch: lab or notebook. It then stores this as an environment variable
so that the final installer steps can launch it. 

We do this up front, so that we can see which packages that user has 
installed before installing Mito.

classes.name: mitoinstaller.installer_steps.installer_step.InstallerStep
documentation:
An installer step is a specific step in an install/upgrade process,
that currently has a name and a function that executes when it runs.

If you label a step as optional, then although this step may fail,
it will not stop the InstallerSteps after it from executing.

NOTE: install steps themselves should not log anything. All the logging
is handeled by the run_installer_steps function, which dramatically
clarifies when things go wrong.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.installer_steps.installer_step_utils.run_installer_steps
documentation:
A helper function for running a list of InstallerStep. This function
handles the running, communication and logging of the steps. 

## On Reporting to the User

This function runs installer steps in a seperate thread, so that it can 
provide continual progress updates to the user about long-running operations.

## On Logging

Our logging strategy meets the following form:
1. If the user is a pro user, we log nothing. Duh. 
2. Otherwise, if the user is not a pro user:
    -   When the installer is started, we always generate `install_started` log
    -   On the "Install mitosheet package" step, we log success, generating a
        'install_mitosheet_package_success' log.
    -   If the installer fails, we generate a log specific to the log step that
        failed, as well as a `install_failed` log. 

Thus, if there is an `install_stared` log, and `install_mitosheet_package_success` log, we can
conclude that install finished correctly. 

If there is an `install_started` log, and and `install_failed` log, then we can conclude
that the install failed. 

Otherwise, if there is an `install_started` log and no `install_mitosheet_package_success`, 
then the install never finished!

Notably, we cannot log if the entire install process finishes correctly 
because we attempt to replace the installer process with  JupyterLab, 
which means we can no longer log.

This cleanup and consistency in logging is primarily meant to make debugging
installer issues easier!

functions.name: mitoinstaller.installer_steps.final_installer_steps.replace_process_with_jupyter
documentation:
Switch the currently running process with a running lab or notebook
instance, depending on which we think the user would prefer.

If we are running tests, then we do not launch JLab. 
# TODO: we don't want to launch this if we're inside a Docker script?

functions.name: mitoinstaller.installer_steps.final_installer_steps.print_success_message
documentation:
Prints a success message to the user, in the case that we cannot
launch JupyterLab. TODO: Furthermore, logs that the jupyter lab instance
could not be launched.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.installer_steps.mitosheet_installer_steps.install_step_mitosheet_check_dependencies
documentation:
This is the most complex step in the installation process. It's
goal is to check if the users existing installation can safely
be upgraded to JLab 3.0. 

To do this, it checks a variety of conditions, mostly around what
version of JLab they have installed, and if this version of JLab has
any installed dependencies (that we cannot safely upgrade).

functions.name: mitoinstaller.installer_steps.mitosheet_installer_steps.remove_mitosheet_3_if_present
documentation:
Because of our changes to the package hierarchy, if the user
currently has mitosheet3 installed, we need to uninstall it,
as the user is moving to mitosheet instead. 

Otherwise, if the user has mitosheet and mitosheet3 installed,
they will get an error launching jlab because it will lead to 
two different extensions getting registered with the same
name.

functions.name: mitoinstaller.install.do_install
documentation:
Runs the installer steps to install the `mitosheet` package.

Notably, the process for installing Mito initially and upgrading Mito are
identical. As such, we reuse this function to upgrade, just with different
error and logging messages.

functions.name: tests.conftest.clear_user_json
documentation:
This fixture reads in the original user.json file that exists before these tests are
run, deletes it, and then recreates it at the end. This allows us to test what happens 
when the user.json file is in various states of out of date and disrepair.

It also turns off the sending of logging to make sure we don't generate a huge number
of new accounts on the frontend.

functions.name: tests.conftest.venv
documentation:
A fixture that you can use to test
various parts of the mitoinstaller package
in an isolated environment.

methods.name: tests.conftest.VirtualEnvironment.get_jupyterlab_extension_names
documentation: Helper function that returns a list of installed extensions
